description.en: Exploit Gemini's two signature strengths — million-token-class context windows and native multimodality across video, audio and documents — while learning the cost, latency and quality caveats that keep RAG relevant.

story_variants.en:
## Two Bets That Paid Off

While rivals raced on reasoning benchmarks, Google placed two structural bets on Gemini: context windows an order of magnitude larger than the competition, and **native multimodality** — one model trained from the start on text, images, audio and video together, rather than a language model with a vision adapter bolted on. By the Gemini 2.x era both bets defined the platform's identity: when a workload involves *hours of video*, *entire codebases* or *thousand-page document sets*, Gemini is usually the first model architects shortlist.

This lesson is about using those superpowers *well* — which means understanding exactly where they stop being magic.

## Long Context: What a Million Tokens Buys

A million-token-class window (with Pro-class variants historically reaching further) translates to roughly: a small codebase, several novels' worth of text, hours of transcript, or a large batch of contracts — *in one prompt*. Genuinely new workload shapes follow:

- **Whole-corpus synthesis**: "read all forty contracts and produce a clause-divergence matrix" — no retrieval step to miss a document.
- **Codebase-scale reasoning**: architecture reviews and cross-file refactor planning over the actual repository.
- **Long-media understanding**: full meeting recordings or lecture series analyzed end-to-end.

Now the caveats, because professionals bill for caveats:

1. **Cost scales linearly with what you send.** A million input tokens on every call is a pricing decision, not just a technical one. **Context caching** — Gemini's mechanism for paying once to load stable content and referencing it cheaply across calls — is the difference between viable and absurd economics for repeated queries over the same corpus.
2. **Latency scales too.** Very long prompts take materially longer to process; interactive UX over huge contexts needs caching, streaming and expectation management.
3. **Attention is not uniform.** Needle-in-a-haystack retrieval tests score near-perfectly, but *synthesis* quality across the full window is a harder, less-solved problem. Content buried mid-context can be under-weighted. Evaluate your task shape, not the vendor's demo shape.

> **Callout:** Long context did not kill RAG — it changed RAG's job. Retrieval remains the right default for cost and latency at scale; long context is the tool for when relationships *between* documents matter, or when building a retrieval pipeline costs more than the problem justifies. The mature architecture uses both: retrieve a candidate set, then reason over it in one large, cached context.

## Multimodal: Documents, Screens, Audio, Video

"Native" multimodality shows up in practice as breadth and uniformity — the same API shape handles:

| Input | Enterprise use case | Note |
|---|---|---|
| Images | Damage photos, dashboards, diagrams | All three vendors are strong here |
| PDFs/documents | Layout-aware contract & invoice reading | Visual layout + text together |
| Audio | Call-center QA, meeting analysis | Direct understanding, not just transcription |
| Video | Training-content indexing, incident review | Gemini's clearest differentiator |

Video is the standout: Gemini processes it as sampled frames plus the audio track, consuming context proportional to duration — which is precisely why video workloads and long context are one story, not two. An hour of footage only fits because the window is enormous; it only *re-fits* affordably because of caching. Claude and GPT models handle images and documents well (and OpenAI's stack is strong on speech), but hours-long native video understanding remains the capability rivals chase.

Practical habits: file-based inputs go through an upload API rather than inline payloads; resolution and sampling settings trade fidelity for tokens; and multimodal outputs still deserve the same validation discipline as text — a model can misread a chart as fluently as it misreads a sentence.

## The Packing Problem

Even a giant window is finite, and enterprise corpora are not. Production long-context systems therefore always contain a humble component: a **context packer** that decides what makes the cut. Given documents with token estimates and a budget, walk the priority-ordered list, include what fits, skip what does not, and report utilization. Greedy packing is crude — smarter systems chunk, summarize or re-rank — but the greedy version is the baseline everything else is measured against, and it is exactly what you will build in the challenge: deterministic, budget-respecting, order-preserving.

> **Adoption pitfall:** Teams discover the big window and stop *curating* inputs entirely — shoveling wikis, logs and email archives in "because it fits." Result: higher bills, slower responses, and answers polluted by irrelevant context. The window is capacity, not advice. Curation still wins evals.

## Where This Leaves You

Treat Gemini's superpowers as a new tier in your architecture toolbox, not a replacement for the old tiers: retrieval for everyday queries, long context for cross-document synthesis and media, caching whenever the same corpus is queried twice. Next lesson, the same models move into Gmail, Docs and Meet — where the context window becomes your colleagues' working day.

description.en: Long context enables new workload shapes; caching makes them affordable; RAG and packing keep them disciplined.

left.label: It fits, so ship the whole wiki every call

right.label: Curate and pack by priority; cache stable corpora; retrieve when synthesis isn't needed

description.en: The window is capacity, not advice — curation still wins on cost, latency and answer quality.

explanation.en: The budget is the context window minus room reserved for instructions and output.
Greedy rule: include a document only if it fits in the remaining budget.
Track utilization — the number that decides whether caching pays for itself.
Skipped documents are visible, not silent: auditability beats mystery truncation.

description.en: A greedy packing run at 99% utilization — note the skip-and-continue behavior when a document doesn't fit.
The production long-context loop: pay to load once, query cheaply many times.

instruction.en: Implement the context-window packer at the heart of every long-context system. Complete pack(docs, budget): iterate (name, tokens) in the given priority order; if the document fits in the remaining budget, include it and print 'packed <name> (<tokens>)'; otherwise print 'skipped <name> (<tokens>)' and CONTINUE to later documents (a smaller one may still fit). Return the total tokens used. After packing DOCS into a 1,000,000-token budget, print a final line 'used=<used> remaining=<budget - used>'. EXACTLY five lines total. Greedy skip-and-continue packing is the baseline every smarter chunking or re-ranking strategy is measured against.

practice_challenges.starter_code:
DOCS = [
    ("contracts", 400_000),
    ("codebase", 500_000),
    ("wiki", 300_000),
    ("logs", 90_000),
]

BUDGET = 1_000_000

def pack(docs, budget):
    used = 0
    # TODO: for each (name, tokens) in order:
    #   - if it fits (used + tokens <= budget): add to used,
    #     print "packed <name> (<tokens>)"
    #   - else: print "skipped <name> (<tokens>)" and keep going
    return used

used = pack(DOCS, BUDGET)
# TODO: print "used=<used> remaining=<BUDGET - used>"
practice_challenges.expected_output:
packed contracts (400000)
packed codebase (500000)
skipped wiki (300000)
packed logs (90000)
used=990000 remaining=10000

en: The fit test is used + tokens <= budget — do NOT stop at the first skip; later smaller docs may still fit.
Print token counts without separators: f"packed {name} ({tokens})" prints 400000, not 400_000.
wiki (300k) fails because 900k + 300k > 1M, but logs (90k) still fits: 900k + 90k = 990k.

practice_challenges.test_code:
import io, contextlib
buf = io.StringIO()
with contextlib.redirect_stdout(buf):
    u = pack(DOCS, BUDGET)
assert u == 990_000
assert buf.getvalue().splitlines() == ["packed contracts (400000)", "packed codebase (500000)", "skipped wiki (300000)", "packed logs (90000)"]
with contextlib.redirect_stdout(io.StringIO()):
    assert pack([], 100) == 0
    assert pack([("a", 101)], 100) == 0

q.en: Why didn't million-token context windows make RAG obsolete?

en: Retrieval still wins on cost and latency for everyday queries; long context earns its bill when cross-document synthesis is required
RAG is contractually required by cloud providers
Long context windows cannot hold documents

q.en: What makes repeated querying over the same huge corpus economically viable on Gemini?

en: Output tokens become free above 100K input
Context caching — pay to load stable content once, reference it cheaply across calls
Compression of the prompt into embeddings
Batch mode doubles the context window

q.en: Which multimodal capability most clearly differentiates Gemini from Claude and GPT as of 2025-2026?

en: Native understanding of long videos (frames plus audio) within huge contexts

q.en: In greedy context packing, why must you continue past a document that doesn't fit?

en: To raise an error for each oversized document
Because the budget resets after every skip
Because a smaller later document may still fit in the remaining budget
Because documents must be packed alphabetically
